subject: Physics
question_text: A 5 kg box is pushed horizontally on a frictionless surface with a net force of 20 N. What is the acceleration of the box?
markscheme:
Using Newton's Second Law: F = ma
a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m s-2 [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: A ball is launched horizontally from the top of a cliff 45 m high with an initial horizontal velocity of 15 m s<sup>-1</sup>. Assuming no air resistance and g = 10 m s<sup>-2</sup>, calculate: (a) the time to reach the ground, (b) the horizontal distance travelled, (c) the speed of the ball just before impact.
markscheme:
(a) Time to reach ground
Using s = ut + ½at²: 45 = 0 + ½(10)t² [1]
t² = 9 → t = 3.0 s [1]

(b) Horizontal distance
x = vx × t = 15 × 3.0 = 45 m [1]

(c) Speed at impact
Vertical velocity: vy = u + at = 0 + 10 × 3.0 = 30 m s-1 [1]
Horizontal velocity: vx = 15 m s-1 (unchanged) [1]
Speed = √(vx² + vy²) = √(15² + 30²) = √(225 + 900) = √1125 ≈ 33.5 m s-1 [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: A 2 kg ball is dropped from rest at a height of 5 m. Using conservation of energy, calculate its speed just before hitting the ground. Take g = 10 m s<sup>-2</sup>.
markscheme:
Using conservation of energy: GPE = KE
mgh = ½mv² [1]
v² = 2gh = 2 × 10 × 5 = 100 m2 s-2 [1]
v = 10 m s-1 [1]
(Note: mass cancels, so answer is independent of mass)

subject: Physics
question_text: A transverse wave has a frequency of 5 Hz and a wavelength of 4 m. What is its speed?
markscheme:
Using the wave equation: v = fλ
v = 5 × 4 = 20 m s-1 [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: State the principle of superposition of waves and explain the difference between constructive and destructive interference.
markscheme:
Principle of Superposition: When two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement at any point is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements at that point. [1]

Constructive interference: waves are in phase (path difference = nλ); displacements add together to give a larger resultant amplitude [1]

Destructive interference: waves are antiphase (path difference = (n+½)λ); displacements partially or completely cancel [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: In a double-slit experiment, light of wavelength 600 nm is incident on two slits separated by 0.5 mm. An interference pattern is observed on a screen 2.0 m away. (a) Calculate the fringe spacing. (b) Describe what would happen to the fringe spacing if the slit separation were doubled.
markscheme:
(a) Fringe spacing
Using: w = λD/d [1]
w = (600 × 10-9 × 2.0) / (0.5 × 10-3) [1]
w = (1.2 × 10-6) / (5 × 10-4)
w = 2.4 × 10-3 m = 2.4 mm [1]

(b) Doubling slit separation:
w ∝ 1/d, so doubling d would halve the fringe spacing [1]
New fringe spacing = 2.4/2 = 1.2 mm [1]
The pattern would have narrower, more closely spaced fringes [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: A resistor has a potential difference of 12 V across it and a current of 0.5 A flowing through it. What is its resistance?
markscheme:
Using Ohm's Law: V = IR
R = V/I = 12/0.5 = 24 Ω [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: State Kirchhoff's two laws and give the physical principle each is based on.
markscheme:
Kirchhoff's First Law (KCL): The sum of currents entering a junction equals the sum of currents leaving it (ΣIin = ΣIout) [1]
Based on: conservation of charge [1]

Kirchhoff's Second Law (KVL): The algebraic sum of EMFs around any closed loop equals the algebraic sum of the potential differences (voltage drops) [1]
Based on: conservation of energy [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: Two resistors of resistance 3 Ω and 6 Ω are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel to a 12 V battery. For each arrangement, calculate the total resistance, total current from the battery, and the power dissipated in the 3 Ω resistor.
markscheme:
(a) Series
Total R = 3 + 6 = 9 Ω [1]
Total current I = V/R = 12/9 = 1.33 A [1]
P in 3 Ω = I²R = (1.33)² × 3 = 5.33 W [1]

(b) Parallel
1/R = 1/3 + 1/6 = 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 → R = 2 Ω [1]
Total current I = V/R = 12/2 = 6 A [1]
V across 3 Ω = 12 V (same in parallel)
P in 3 Ω = V²/R = 144/3 = 48 W [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: State the equation of state for an ideal gas and define each symbol.
markscheme:
pV = nRT [1]
Where:
• p = pressure (Pa or N m-2) [1]
• V = volume (m3)
• n = number of moles (mol)
• R = molar gas constant = 8.31 J K-1 mol-1
• T = absolute temperature (K) [1]
(accept symbol definitions without units for 1 mark each, max 3)

subject: Physics
question_text: A gas occupies a volume of 2.0 × 10<sup>-3</sup> m<sup>3</sup> at a pressure of 1.0 × 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and temperature of 300 K. The gas is compressed to a volume of 5.0 × 10<sup>-4</sup> m<sup>3</sup> at the same temperature. Calculate the new pressure.
markscheme:
Using Boyle's Law (constant T): p1V1 = p2V2 [1]
p2 = p1V1/V2 [1]
p2 = (1.0 × 105 × 2.0 × 10-3) / (5.0 × 10-4)
p2 = 4.0 × 105 Pa [1]

subject: Physics
question_text: Which type of radiation is stopped by a sheet of paper?
markscheme: Alpha (α) particles are the most ionising but least penetrating radiation. They are stopped by a few centimetres of air or a sheet of paper. This is because they have a large charge (+2) and mass, causing frequent ionisation. [1]
